plan.md.text: one thing that we are missing in this is adding types or overwriting. this is where [code] comes in, where users can do something like '- load mynumbers.dll', it detects the what interface it implements and injects it, allowing users to overwrite what 'int' is or add their new type
plan.md.author: user

plan.md.text:
Big one — captured as a new spine section 'Extending the type vocabulary at runtime', and detailed in dispatch.md 'Runtime-loaded and overwritten types'. The good news: most of it is already wired. `code.load` (PLang/app/modules/code/load.cs) already does load→scan-for-interface→register for ICode providers; type-loading is the same shape pointed at [PlangType] classes. `Registry.RegisterRuntime(name, type)` is the existing hook, and `Registry.ResolveType` already checks runtime registrations BEFORE the built-in table — so overwriting 'int' works at the resolution layer for free.

One honest limit I want you to see: runtime registration changes *resolution and rendering* (what 'int' maps to, how it serializes) but it can't rewrite what the source generator already baked at build — the PLNG slot validation, the Data<int> params on already-compiled handlers, the type stamps in shipped .pr files. So `- load myint.dll` makes int resolve/render differently going forward, but a handler compiled against built-in int still sees built-in int at its typed slot. Adding NEW types is unconstrained; overwriting built-ins is 'new resolution + new rendering, same compiled slots.' Flagged so nobody expects load to retroactively rewrite already-built goals. The serializer side: a loaded DLL ships an ITypeRenderer per format, registered into the same dispatch table the generator feeds.
plan.md.author: architect

plan.md.text: we cant to 'to console' or 'to html' it doesnt make sense, the state the code is running controls that, so if it´s a web request the serializer will be html serializer, if he is running in console it´s text serializer
plan.md.author: user

plan.md.anchor: **Runtime, step 2.** `output.write` grabs `%photo%`, hands the Data to the console channel's writer with serializer identity `text/plain`. The channel does **not** look inside `Value`. It calls `data.Normalize(...)` to walk the graph; Normalize encounters the Image instance, sees `IWireWritable`, asks it to render for `serializer.Type = "text/plain"`. Image's implementation dispatches on mime: text/plain → `writer.String("[image: image/png 1.2MB]")` (or the originating path, if held). Console gets a readable line.

plan.md.anchor: **Runtime, step 3.** Same flow, serializer identity `text/html`. Image dispatches: text/html → `writer.String("")`. HTML channel gets the markup. (Today `text/html` is aliased to `application/json`'s writer in the registry — HTML markup ships as a JSON string field. When HTML grows its own writer, the raw-string slot is the one the type's `IWireWritable.WriteTo` already targets; no type-side change needed.)

plan.md.anchor: 1. **Interface location and signature.** Leaning `app/data/IWireWritable.cs` (sits next to `IBooleanResolvable`, since `Data.Normalize` is the dispatcher). Signature: `void WriteTo(IWriter writer, ISerializer serializer)` — gives the value both the encoder and the format identity. Sync because format encoding shouldn't reach I/O. Alternative: pass just the mime string instead of the serializer instance — leaner, but loses the ability to delegate sub-fragments back through the serializer (which Image might want for properties).
plan.md.author: user

plan.md.text:
Agree the per-serializer-file shape is more OBP than one-method-switches-on-mime. Proposal: `app/types/<name>/serializer/<format>.cs` per (type, format), each file owns one rendering. Source generator scans them and emits a static dispatch table keyed on (Data.Type, writer.Format). Writer carries its format identity (`json.Writer.Format = "application/json"`); Wire.Write does the lookup once at the value slot and calls the registered static method. No interface on the value, no internal mime switch. Normalize stays as the fallback walk for non-registered types (today's domain decomposition).

Cost: ~3 types × ~5 formats × ~5 file lines = ~75 tiny single-method files. Each one is one decision in one place. Trade is fewer-files vs `ls` reading like a manifest of what the type can do per output medium. I lean per-file; final call is yours. If you sign off, I'll rewrite plan/dispatch.md and the folder tree commits to it, and the open question collapses.
plan.md.anchor: this.Normalize.cs                (existing) — walk fallback, untouched for IWireWritable types
plan.md.author: architect

plan.md.anchor: 2. **Type registry shape.** Two options. *Discovery-time* (the source generator scans `app/types/<name>/this.cs` and emits a static registration table at compile time — matches how `[Action]` discovery already works). *App-construction-time* (App scans loaded assemblies for `[PlangType]` once at startup — matches how `path.scheme.@this` populates today). Discovery-time is faster and lets PLNG-style gates catch missing registrations at build; App-time is simpler and matches the existing scheme registry. I lean discovery-time but it's not a hill.

plan.md.anchor: 4. **HTML as its own writer vs. JSON-aliased.** Today `text/html` aliases `application/json` in the serializer registry. The `` markup case for image works around this by riding as a JSON string field. If we let HTML grow its own writer on this branch, every type's `IWireWritable.WriteTo` for `text/html` writes raw markup (no JSON escaping). Cleaner long-term; ships HTML as a real format. But it widens scope. Defer to a follow-up?

plan/dispatch.md.anchor: Sync. Reaching for I/O during wire emission is a bug — the value should already hold its bytes (or its lazy-resolved equivalent) by the time it reaches the writer. Async dispatch lives at action verbs, where `image.resize` may read source bytes from disk; the result of that action is a fully-materialized `Image` instance with `Bytes` populated.
plan/dispatch.md.author: user

plan/dispatch.md.text: returning the writer should be fine, dont see a problem with that
plan/dispatch.md.anchor: The complication: `Normalize` produces a *tree of primitives* that `Wire.Write` then walks. `IWireWritable.WriteTo` doesn't return primitives — it writes directly into the `IWriter`. So Normalize can't return the rendered content; it has to return a *deferred marker* that `json.Writer` (and future writers) recognize.
plan/dispatch.md.author: user

plan/dispatch.md.text: With the per-serializer-file shape I'm proposing on plan.md line 84, the A-vs-B question goes away — there's no marker-and-Normalize handshake. Wire.Write does the `(Data.Type, writer.Format) → static method` lookup directly at the value slot and calls it; non-registered types fall through to today's Normalize walk. I'll rewrite this whole section once you sign off on the shape; leaving open until then.

plan/dispatch.md.author: architect

plan/dispatch.md.text: I think I need to see some flow to understand this
plan/dispatch.md.anchor: - **Option A — Deferred marker.** Normalize returns a `WireWritableNode { Value }` placeholder. The writer's value-dispatch method recognizes it and calls `Value.WriteTo(this, serializer)`. Clean separation; needs the writer to learn one new node type.
plan/dispatch.md.author: user

plan/dispatch.md.text:
Flow under the per-serializer-file shape (proposed on plan.md line 84). Wire.Write seeing `Data{Type=image, Value=<Image>}`:

1. Read Data.Type → 'image'.
2. Read writer.Format → 'application/json' (or 'text/plain', etc. — the writer carries its own format identity).
3. Registry lookup `(image, application/json) → app.types.image.serializer.json.Write` (the static method generated from the file).
4. Call `Write(image, writer)`. Inside that file: `writer.String(Convert.ToBase64String(image.Bytes))`.
5. Done — no Normalize hop, no marker placeholder, no internal mime switch.

If lookup misses (Data.Type is unregistered, or the type didn't author a serializer for this format), fall back to today's `data.Normalize() → walker → property bag` path. I'll fold this concrete trace into the rewritten dispatch.md once you sign off on the shape.
plan/dispatch.md.anchor: - **Option A — Deferred marker.** Normalize returns a `WireWritableNode { Value }` placeholder. The writer's value-dispatch method recognizes it and calls `Value.WriteTo(this, serializer)`. Clean separation; needs the writer to learn one new node type.

The concrete 5-step flow is in the rewritten 'The flow, concretely' section.

plan/review-opus-4-8.md.text: but for regular folks it's the same, so I think it should be by default equal but a setting where you can change that
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.anchor: The fix is **exact-only cross-kind equality**. `decimal(0.1m) == double(0.1d)` is *false*: 0.1 has no exact double representation, so they're not the same number under any precision-respecting view. Same for 0.1000000000000000055m vs 0.1d. All three become inequal. Transitivity restored.
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.author: user

plan/review-opus-4-8.md.text:
Conceded back — your call wins. The right shape is:

- **`==` / `Equals` is lenient by default.** Cross-kind promotes to a common form and compares. Matches what every regular PLang dev expects: `0.1 == 0.1` is true, period, regardless of whether one side originated as decimal and the other as double.
- **`ExactEquals(other)` for those who know.** Explicit method. Same Kind + exact bit-level. No ambient state, no policy axis — the user reaches for the method when they need it (crypto, finance, debugging).
- **`GetHashCode` follows the lenient `==`.** Canonical form: lenient-equal values hash to the same bucket. Cost is hash collisions for nearby-but-not-equal values; bucket lookup is still O(1) amortized — same trade Python takes with `hash(0.1) == hash(Decimal('0.1'))`.

The transitivity hazard in HashSet/Dictionary is still real in theory but bounded in practice: PLang devs almost never put `number` directly in a HashSet — they use list / dict actions whose equality discipline is whatever PLang's collection layer enforces. So the edge case sits in C#-only code paths that opt into ExactEquals.

Withdrawing my 'full concede' on Opus 4.8's point 2 — going lenient default, opt-in strict.
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.anchor: The fix is **exact-only cross-kind equality**. `decimal(0.1m) == double(0.1d)` is *false*: 0.1 has no exact double representation, so they're not the same number under any precision-respecting view. Same for 0.1000000000000000055m vs 0.1d. All three become inequal. Transitivity restored.
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.author: architect

plan/review-opus-4-8.md.text: problem is, we dont really have a way to convert it to either or other type, so thats why I want the default to be equal and those that know what they are doing set it
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.anchor: **The cost we accept:** `From(0.1m) != From(0.1)` will surprise developers who *think of them as the same number*. But that surprise is the truth — 0.1m IS NOT 0.1d in any honest precision view. Surfacing it as inequality is honest; silently producing wrong answers in HashSet/Dictionary is not.
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.author: user

plan/review-opus-4-8.md.text: Same thread as the L41 reply. The 'can't convert' framing is exactly why lenient is the right default — devs don't know if their `0.1` is decimal or double and shouldn't have to. Resolved together with the L41 comment.
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.anchor: **The cost we accept:** `From(0.1m) != From(0.1)` will surprise developers who *think of them as the same number*. But that surprise is the truth — 0.1m IS NOT 0.1d in any honest precision view. Surfacing it as inequality is honest; silently producing wrong answers in HashSet/Dictionary is not.
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.author: architect

plan/review-opus-4-8.md.text: everthing in plang returns data so, not sure what you mean, maybe some internal thing, private method would throw, but we should return Data error to the runtime from the module.
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.anchor: **My position: concede, with a clean split.** Real architectural mismatch. The fix isn't "everything returns Data" — C# operators (`+`, `*`, etc.) can't, by signature. The split is by surface:
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.author: user

plan/review-opus-4-8.md.text:
Right — that's the split I'm proposing, just phrased from the C# side. The handler / module-surface boundary always returns `Data` — never throws. Internal `private` methods (and C# operators on `number` itself like `+`, `-`, `*`) throw the way every CLR numeric does; the math handler catches and converts to `Data.Error`. So:

- `math.add` handler: `Run() → Data<number>`. Calls `number.Add(a, b, policy)` (the Data-returning named method, not the operator). Catches happen inside `number.Add` itself.
- `number.Add(...)` body: catches `OverflowException` internally, returns `Data<number>.Fail("MathOverflow", ...)`.
- `number + number` operator: throws on overflow. Used by `number.Add` internally for the lenient path, and by any C# code outside the action layer that wants ergonomic arithmetic.

Boundary up to the runtime is Data; the back of the curtain has exceptions for the same reason `decimal + decimal` does. Confirming we're aligned.
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.anchor: **My position: concede, with a clean split.** Real architectural mismatch. The fix isn't "everything returns Data" — C# operators (`+`, `*`, etc.) can't, by signature. The split is by surface:
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.author: architect

plan/review-opus-4-8.md.text: Maybe we dont need context, but it is thread safe so not sure what the problem is.
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.anchor: **My position: full concede.** Mutable Context on a value-typed numeric is wrong. I bolted it on for cosmetic factory-shape uniformity with `path.Resolve(raw, context)`. The cost wasn't visible to me at write-time but is real:
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.author: user

plan/review-opus-4-8.md.text:
Fair pushback. The thread-safety isn't the problem (and Opus 4.8 didn't quite frame it as one either) — it's that Context on a `number` has no real use site after construction. The factory `Resolve(raw, context)` uses it briefly for registry / catalog lookup during construction, then has nothing to do with it. Storing it on the instance is carrying unused state, and the OBP-#4 risk only matters if number gets cached cross-request — which it might, since values get parked in MemoryStack.

Fix is mechanical, not architectural alarm: `Resolve(raw, context)` keeps the parameter (signature-uniform with path.Resolve, etc.) but the body doesn't write it to a field; `From(5)` and internal factories don't see Context at all. Drop the `Context { get; set; }` property and the `IContext` interface on number. No state, no risk, no perf hit on construction. Mechanical enough that it lands without further discussion.
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.anchor: **My position: full concede.** Mutable Context on a value-typed numeric is wrong. I bolted it on for cosmetic factory-shape uniformity with `path.Resolve(raw, context)`. The cost wasn't visible to me at write-time but is real:
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.author: architect

plan/review-opus-4-8.md.text: Landed as readonly struct (named @this, convention intact) in storage.md. One correction you should see, since your reason was allocation: I verified Data.Value is `object` (app/data/this.cs:86), so a struct boxes the moment it enters Data — which is the dominant runtime path (variables, memory stack, results). The allocation win is real only for pure-C# arithmetic intermediates that never touch Data (a reducer accumulator like list.sum over a big list), and that path is partly mooted because heavy numeric loops route to [code] anyway. Struct still stands on value-semantics grounds (number IS a value, like int/decimal), so I proceeded — but the allocation rationale specifically is weak. If that changes your mind it's a one-keyword flip back to sealed class. Recorded in the 'Decisions landed' table.
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.anchor: ## What 4.8 didn't address that I think it should have
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.author: architect

plan/review-opus-4-8.md.text:
OK let me lay it out concretely so we have something to talk against.

**The case for `readonly record struct number(NumberKind Kind, long I, decimal D, double F)`:**

1. **Exact equality auto-generated** (record struct gives field-by-field). Matches the ExactEquals we already need on the lenient-default side — just inherit the struct's auto-gen and use that as ExactEquals; override Equals for the lenient default.
2. **No heap allocation per number.** Today's class form heap-allocates one `@this` per arithmetic result; struct sits on the stack / inline in `Data<number>.Value`. Heavy-math goals (a loop summing 10k numbers) save ~10k allocations.
3. **Matches what `int` / `decimal` / `double` already are** — value types. The surface reads coherently: every numeric in the CLR is a struct; PLang's `number` joins them.
4. **OBP convention is preserved where it matters.** `@this` is for navigable owner types (Goal, Step, Config) — types where `parent.child.grandchild` is the language. A `number` has nothing to navigate to. The convention costs us nothing here because we're not losing navigability.

**The cost:**

1. **Breaks the `@this` convention for one class.** `app.types.number` (struct) vs `app.types.number.@this` (class) is a real readability inconsistency across the codebase. Everything else under `app/types/` would use `@this`; only number wouldn't.
2. **`IBooleanResolvable.AsBooleanAsync()` boxes the struct on interface dispatch.** Every truthiness check (`if %x%`) allocates. This partially defeats the no-heap win — though arithmetic (the hot path) doesn't go through interface dispatch, so arithmetic stays alloc-free; only truthiness pays.
3. **Source-generator coupling.** PLNG001 expects `Data<T>` parameter slots; T as struct is supported (`Data<int>` works today), but `[PlangType]` discovery and the registry plumbing would need to handle the struct case symmetrically with classes. Probably fine; needs a quick check.
4. **`partial` files under the type folder get awkward.** `app/types/number/this.cs` for the struct definition, then `this.Parse.cs`, `this.Operators.cs`, `this.Arithmetic.cs` are partials *of the struct*. C# allows partial structs but it's less common; reads slightly differently than partial classes.

**My read:** the wins (alloc-free arithmetic, auto-equality, value-type honesty) outweigh the costs (one convention break, truthiness boxing). The convention break is the load-bearing concern — but the convention exists for a reason, and that reason doesn't apply here.

**What I'd want to know from you:** how strongly do you feel about preserving `@this` everywhere vs. accepting one structural exception for the pure-value case? If you're firm on @this everywhere, the class form stays and we accept the heap allocation + manual equality + manual hash; if you're open to the exception, the struct form is cleaner. Leaving open.
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.author: architect

plan/review-opus-4-8.md.text: I think the win for less allocation is more important. so lets try for struct setup on this on type
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.author: user

plan/review-opus-4-8.md.text: Agreed — Option B (separate `rational` sibling type, parallel to number and the future quantity), added later with a real consumer driving it, not this branch. Recorded in the 'Decisions landed' table and flagged in plan.md open questions so the vocabulary stays forward-coherent. Nothing to build now.

plan/review-opus-4-8.md.author: architect

plan/review-opus-4-8.md.text: one thing I thought of is, what if we support rational numbers? so people do '- set %x% = 7/8' and it stays that way?
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.anchor: **The one thing I'd want to discuss before changing anything**: the **sealed-class-vs-readonly-record-struct** question. 4.8 didn't raise it directly but the equality and Context critiques both reinforce the case. Worth a 10-minute back-and-forth before either decision is committed to the doc.
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.author: user

plan/review-opus-4-8.md.text:
Good question. Two clean ways to land it:

**Option A — `Rational` as a sixth `NumberKind` inside `number`.** Adds two slots (`long _num`, `long _den`) or shares `_i` for numerator with a new `_den` slot. `Parse("7/8")` lands as `Kind = Rational`. Arithmetic: rational + rational stays rational (lowest-terms canonicalization on construction via GCD). Mixed-kind ops promote: rational + decimal → decimal (with loss measured by the lcm of the denominator); rational + double → double (IEEE). Same Data<number> wrapper, same handlers, same wire shape. The cost: one more row/column on the promotion table, plus the rational-specific reductions (GCD, lowest-terms invariant).

**Option B — `rational` as a separate sibling type** under `app/types/rational/`. Parallel to `number`, parallel to the future `quantity` (which handles `5kg`). Has its own arithmetic, its own truthiness (`0/n` falsy), its own serializer files. Cross-type ops between `number` and `rational` go through explicit promotion (`(number)%r%`) — the LLM picks the type per the developer's prose intent (`- set %price% = 7/8` keys on the slash being kept exact, picks rational; `- set %x% = 7/8` with general numeric intent picks number).

**My instinct: Option B.** Three reasons:

1. Rational arithmetic has different rules (GCD on construction, denominator-overflow questions, no IEEE concepts). Folding it into `number` widens every `number` switch with one more case, and the `Parse` rules grow another fork.
2. Most PLang devs don't reach for rational. Putting it in the universal umbrella costs every handler that touches numbers an extra case to consider.
3. The pattern matches `quantity` (also numbers-with-extra-structure). Both sit as siblings, not inside `number`. Coherent vocabulary.

**The pull toward Option A:** the worked example `- set %x% = 7/8` reads more naturally if `%x%(number)` covers it — devs don't have to know they wanted `rational` specifically. They'd just write the literal and the LLM would type-stamp it.

The right call depends on whether you see rational as 'a kind of number' (A) or 'a kind of numeric thing parallel to number' (B). Quantity is clearly (B) because units are non-numeric structure; rational is borderline.

Not a this-branch decision either way — both are deferrable to a follow-up branch with a real consumer driving them (the same discipline we applied to BigInteger and quantity). But worth knowing your read so the type vocabulary inventory is forward-coherent. Leaving open.
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.anchor: **The one thing I'd want to discuss before changing anything**: the **sealed-class-vs-readonly-record-struct** question. 4.8 didn't raise it directly but the equality and Context critiques both reinforce the case. Worth a 10-minute back-and-forth before either decision is committed to the doc.
plan/review-opus-4-8.md.author: architect

plan/review-opus-4-8.md.text: I think we would go for B then, and the good thing is, we dont need to implement now, that can be added later, so lets add it later
